message.content:
Feature: MHPCO Claim Office - policy management system with quote (compute premium) and claim (process damage) operations, exposed as a CLI.

Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
CLI entry: src/cli.ts

Requirements: See /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-22_08-52-17_claim-office-example-mapping_v4.2-shared-context_opus-4-7-portkey-no-thinking/prompt.md for the full specification, including item pricing, premium modifiers, claim processing rules, rounding rules, edge cases, and the CLI JSON input/output schema.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, no HITL checkpoints.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll start by reading the specification thoroughly.

content.content:
1	# MHPCO Claim Office Kata
2	
3	## Setting
4	
5	You are building the policy management system for the **Most Honorable
6	Privileged Claims Office for Magical Risks and Cursed Items** (MHPCO),
7	an old-fashioned insurance institution that covers magical items —
8	swords, amulets, staves, potions, and components such as runes and
9	moonstones. The MHPCO is bureaucratic, slightly stingy, and proud of
10	its venerable traditions.
11	
12	## Feature
13	
14	The system supports two operations: **quote** computes the premium for
15	a list of items a customer wishes to insure, and **claim** processes a
16	damage report against an existing policy.
17	
18	## Rules
19	
20	### Item values and base premiums
21	
22	Each main item has an insurance value and a base premium according to
23	the MHPCO price list:
24	
25	- Sword: 1000 G insurance value, 100 G base premium
26	- Amulet: 600 G / 60 G
27	- Staff: 800 G / 80 G
28	- Potion: 400 G / 40 G
29	
30	Components — for example runes and moonstones — are insured at 250 G
31	each, with a base premium of 25 G per component. A building block of
32	3 alike components is offered at a special base premium of 60 G.
33	
34	### Premium modifiers
35	
36	- Cursed items add a 50 % risk surcharge.
37	- Highly enchanted items (enchantment level ≥ 5) add a 30 % risk
38	  surcharge.
39	- Long-standing customers (≥ 2 years of business with MHPCO) receive a
40	  20 % loyalty discount.
41	- A first insurance carries a 10 % initial assessment surcharge.
42	- Customers receive a 15 % discount on each contract after their first.
43	- A 5 G processing fee is added to every premium.
44	
45	All amounts are rounded to whole G in the MHPCO's favor.
46	
47	### Claim processing
48	
49	A deductible of 100 G applies per damage event. The total payout per
50	policy is capped at twice the insurance sum.
51	
52	- Damage to items with enchantment level ≥ 8 is reimbursed at 50 % of
53	  the damage amount.
54	- Damage to items made of dragon material is fully reimbursed.
55	
56	## Examples
57	
58	The examples below illustrate single rules in isolation. Values shown
59	are base premiums or raw payout amounts before integration with other
60	modifiers, unless stated otherwise.
61	
62	### Building block of 3 alike components
63	
64	- 2 runes → 50 G base premium
65	- 3 runes → 60 G base premium (block applies)
66	- 4 runes → 100 G base premium (no block — block requires exactly 3)
67	- 7 runes → 175 G base premium
68	
69	### "Alike" components
70	
71	> ❓ Hmm — "alike" components: does that mean exactly the same type,
72	> or just the same family (rune-y, gemstone-y)?
73	
74	- 2 runes + 1 moonstone → 75 G base premium (no block: different types)
75	- 3 runes + 3 moonstones → 120 G base premium (two separate blocks)
76	
77	### Modifier scope on multi-item policies
78	
79	> ❓ A policy with several items: does the cursed surcharge apply to
80	> the whole policy, or just to the cursed item?
81	
82	- a policy covers a cursed sword (base premium 100 G) and a plain
83	  amulet (base premium 60 G) → policy base premium 160 G; the cursed
84	  surcharge adds 50 G (50 % of the cursed sword's base premium, not
85	  of the policy total) → 210 G before further modifiers and fee
86	- item-specific modifiers (cursed, high enchantment) apply to the
87	  base premium of the affected item; policy-wide modifiers (loyalty,
88	  first insurance, follow-up contract) apply to the policy base
89	  premium (the sum of all item base premiums); the processing fee is
90	  added at the very end
91	
92	### Modifier thresholds
93	
94	- customer with exactly 2 years with MHPCO → loyalty discount applies
95	- sword with exactly enchantment 5 → high-enchantment surcharge
96	  applies; if cursed, both surcharges apply
97	- sword with enchantment 4 → no high-enchantment surcharge; curse
98	  surcharge applies only if cursed
99	- dragon-material sword with exactly enchantment 8, damage 1000 G →
100	  payout 400 G (high-enchantment clause applies, then deductible)
101	
102	### Deductible per damage event
103	
104	- a dragon attack damages an insured sword (500 G) and an insured
105	  amulet (300 G); payout = 600 G (the 100 G deductible applies once
106	  per damaged item)
107	
108	### Standard reimbursement (no special clauses)
109	
110	- regular sword (steel, enchantment 3), damage 500 G → payout 400 G
111	  (full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible; no special clause applies)
112	- damage to a rune (insurance value 250 G), damage 200 G → payout
113	  100 G (full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible; runes have no
114	  enchantment level or material, so no special clause applies)
115	
116	### Enchantment threshold vs. dragon material
117	
118	- dragon-material sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G → payout 400 G
119	  (both clauses apply; the 50 % rule wins, then deductible: 500 − 100)
120	- dragon-material sword, enchantment 5, damage 800 G → payout 700 G
121	  (only the dragon-material clause applies: full reimbursement,
122	  then deductible: 800 − 100)
123	- steel sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G → payout 400 G
124	  (only the high-enchantment clause applies: 50 % first, then
125	  deductible: 500 − 100)
126	
127	### Multiple items of the same type
128	
129	> ❓ What if a customer brings two of the same item — one in hand,
130	> one in the backpack? Or just two swords because they fight with
131	> two?
132	
133	- a policy covers two swords → insurance sum 2000 G (= 2×1000),
134	  cap 4000 G
135	- a dragon attack damages both swords; `damages` contains two
136	  `{itemType: "sword", ...}` entries → each entry is treated as a
137	  separate damage with its own deductible
138	- if the `damages` array contains more entries of a given type than
139	  the policy actually covers (e.g. two sword damages but only one
140	  sword insured) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code; the
141	  whole claim is rejected
142	
143	### Cap exhaustion
144	
145	- a policy covers a sword and an amulet → insurance sum 1600 G
146	  (= 1000 + 600, the sum of the items' insurance values), cap 3200 G
147	- a cursed sword (insurance value 1000 G, premium with modifiers
148	  165 G) → cap 2000 G (based on the unmodified insurance value;
149	  premium modifiers do not raise the cap)
150	- a policy covers a sword and 3 runes (a block) → insurance sum
151	  1750 G (= 1000 + 3×250); the block discount affects the premium
152	  only, not the insurance sum
153	- a sword is insured (insurance sum 1000 G, cap 2000 G); two successive
154	  claims of 1500 G each
155	- first claim → payout 1400 G, cap remaining 600 G
156	- second claim → payout 600 G, cap remaining 0 G (the desired 1400 G
157	  is reduced to the remaining cap)
158	
159	### Rounding in the MHPCO's favor
160	
161	- a premium calculation that yields 197.5 G → final premium 198 G
162	  (rounded up)
163	- a payout calculation that yields 350.5 G → final payout 350 G
164	  (rounded down)
165	- intermediate amounts during a calculation are kept as fractions;
166	  only the final premium or payout is rounded
167	
168	### Edge cases
169	
170	- empty item list → premium 5 G (only the processing fee)
171	- quote includes an item with an unknown type (e.g.
172	  `{type: "broomstick"}`) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code
173	  and writes an error description to stderr; no `results` are written
174	  to stdout
175	- claim references a damage entry whose item is not part of the policy
176	  (e.g. an amulet damaged when only a sword is insured, or an item
177	  with an unknown type) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code
178	  and writes an error description to stderr
179	- claim contains a damage entry with `amount: -200` → the CLI exits
180	  with a non-zero status code and writes an error description to
181	  stderr
182	
183	## Integration examples
184	
185	The following two examples combine multiple rules to clarify how
186	modifiers stack and how customer history affects the premium.
187	
188	### Newcomer with a cursed sword
189	
190	- customer: 0 years with MHPCO, no previous contract
191	- item: a cursed sword (steel, enchantment 3)
192	- premium: 165 G
193	- (100 G base + 50 G curse + 10 G first insurance = 160 G + 5 G fee = 165 G)
194	
195	### Long-standing customer's second contract
196	
197	> ❓ Wait — does "first insurance" mean the customer's first ever
198	> contract, or the first time we see this particular item? A
199	> long-standing customer with a brand-new sword: does the first
200	> insurance surcharge still apply?
201	
202	- customer: 3 years with MHPCO; this is the customer's second `quote`
203	  in the scenario
204	- item: a cursed sword (steel, enchantment 7)
205	- premium: 160 G
206	- (100 G base + 50 G curse + 30 G high enchantment − 20 G loyalty
207	  + 10 G first insurance − 15 G follow-up contract = 155 G + 5 G fee
208	  = 160 G)
209	- The first insurance surcharge still applies to the new sword, even
210	  though the customer is on a follow-up contract — each item in a
211	  `quote` is treated as a first insurance, regardless of customer
212	  history.
213	
214	## CLI input/output format
215	
216	The implementation must be exposed as a command-line executable
217	`claim-office`. It reads a JSON document from stdin describing a
218	scenario and writes a JSON document with the corresponding results to
219	stdout.
220	
221	A scenario is a JSON object with a `customer` object and a `steps`
222	array. The `customer` describes the single customer for the whole
223	scenario. Each step has an `op` field (`"quote"` or `"claim"`) and the
224	operation-specific input fields. Steps are processed sequentially;
225	later `claim` steps refer to a policy created by an earlier `quote`
226	step via its zero-based step index in a `policy` field.
227	
228	Stdout receives a JSON object with a `results` array of the same length
229	and order as the input `steps`. Each result contains:
230	
231	- For a `quote` step: `premium` (integer, total premium in G).
232	- For a `claim` step: `payout` (integer, payout in G) and
233	  `remainingCap` (integer, cap remaining on the policy after this
234	  claim).
235	
236	### JSON Schema (normative)
237	
238	The field names below are **binding**. Your implementation must use
239	exactly these names; do not rename or restructure them.
240	
241	**Input (stdin):**
242	
243	```json
244	{
245	  "type": "object",
246	  "required": ["customer", "steps"],
247	  "properties": {
248	    "customer": {
249	      "type": "object",
250	      "required": ["yearsWithMHPCO"],
251	      "properties": {
252	        "yearsWithMHPCO": { "type": "integer" }
253	      }
254	    },
255	    "steps": {
256	      "type": "array",
257	      "items": {
258	        "oneOf": [
259	          {
260	            "description": "Quote step",
261	            "required": ["op", "items"],
262	            "properties": {
263	              "op": { "const": "quote" },
264	              "items": {
265	                "type": "array",
266	                "items": {
267	                  "type": "object",
268	                  "required": ["type"],
269	                  "properties": {
270	                    "type":        { "type": "string", "description": "sword | amulet | staff | potion | rune | moonstone" },
271	                    "material":    { "type": "string" },
272	                    "enchantment": { "type": "integer" },
273	                    "cursed":      { "type": "boolean" }
274	                  }
275	                }
276	              }
277	            }
278	          },
279	          {
280	            "description": "Claim step",
281	            "required": ["op", "policy", "incident"],
282	            "properties": {
283	              "op":     { "const": "claim" },
284	              "policy": { "type": "integer", "description": "zero-based index of the quote step that created the policy" },
285	              "incident": {
286	                "type": "object",
287	                "required": ["cause", "damages"],
288	                "properties": {
289	                  "cause": { "type": "string" },
290	                  "damages": {
291	                    "type": "array",
292	                    "items": {
293	                      "type": "object",
294	                      "required": ["itemType", "amount"],
295	                      "properties": {
296	                        "itemType": { "type": "string" },
297	                        "amount":   { "type": "integer" }
298	                      }
299	                    }
300	                  }
301	                }
302	              }
303	            }
304	          }
305	        ]
306	      }
307	    }
308	  }
309	}
310	```
311	
312	**Output (stdout):**
313	
314	```json
315	{
316	  "type": "object",
317	  "required": ["results"],
318	  "properties": {
319	    "results": {
320	      "type": "array",
321	      "items": {
322	        "oneOf": [
323	          {
324	            "description": "Quote result",
325	            "required": ["premium"],
326	            "properties": {
327	              "premium": { "type": "integer" }
328	            }
329	          },
330	          {
331	            "description": "Claim result",
332	            "required": ["payout", "remainingCap"],
333	            "properties": {
334	              "payout":       { "type": "integer" },
335	              "remainingCap": { "type": "integer" }
336	            }
337	          }
338	        ]
339	      }
340	    }
341	  }
342	}
343	```
344	
345	### Schema example
346	
347	Stdin:
348	
349	```json
350	{
351	  "customer": {"yearsWithMHPCO": 5},
352	  "steps": [
353	    {
354	      "op": "quote",
355	      "items": [
356	        {"type": "amulet", "material": "silver", "enchantment": 2, "cursed": false}
357	      ]
358	    },
359	    {
360	      "op": "claim",
361	      "policy": 0,
362	      "incident": {
363	        "cause": "fire",
364	        "damages": [
365	          {"itemType": "amulet", "amount": 200}
366	        ]
367	      }
368	    }
369	  ]
370	}
371	```
372	
373	Stdout shape:
374	
375	```json
376	{"results": [{"premium": <integer>}, {"payout": <integer>, "remainingCap": <integer>}]}
377	```
378	
379	## Expected Output Files
380	
381	- Source files implementing the logic and the `claim-office` CLI
382	- Test files
383	- CLI entry point at `src/cli.ts` (reads JSON from stdin, writes JSON to stdout)
384	
385	## Constraints
386	
387	- Use TypeScript for the implementation
388	- The CLI must read JSON from stdin and write JSON to stdout
389	- The CLI entry point must be at `src/cli.ts`
390	- Internal data structures and module organization are free
391

content.text: Now I have a complete understanding of the spec. Let me create the example mapping file and then the test list.

input.content:
# MHPCO Claim Office — Example Mapping

## Rules

### R1: Item base values and premiums (price list)

Each "main" item has an insurance value and a base premium:

| Type   | Insurance value (G) | Base premium (G) |
|--------|---------------------|------------------|
| sword  | 1000                | 100              |
| amulet | 600                 | 60               |
| staff  | 800                 | 80               |
| potion | 400                 | 40               |

Components (rune, moonstone, etc.): insurance value 250 G each,
base premium 25 G per component. A "building block" of **exactly 3
alike** components is offered at a special base premium of 60 G
(replaces the 3 × 25 G for those 3 components only).

### R2: Premium modifiers

- **Cursed item:** +50 % surcharge on that item's base premium.
- **Highly enchanted** (enchantment level ≥ 5): +30 % surcharge on
  that item's base premium.
- **Long-standing customer** (yearsWithMHPCO ≥ 2): −20 % loyalty
  discount on the policy base premium (sum of item base premiums).
- **First insurance:** +10 % initial assessment surcharge — applied
  per item, on each item's base premium (every item in a quote is
  treated as a first insurance, regardless of customer history).
- **Follow-up contract** (customer's 2nd or later quote in the
  scenario): −15 % discount on the policy base premium.
- **Processing fee:** flat +5 G added at the very end.

Order of application (per integration examples):
1. Per-item modifiers (curse, high enchantment, first insurance)
   apply to each item's base premium.
2. Policy-wide modifiers (loyalty, follow-up) apply to the policy
   base premium (the sum of item base premiums).
3. The 5 G processing fee is added at the very end.
4. The total is rounded UP to the nearest whole G (in the MHPCO's
   favor).

Concrete: total = round_up(
  sum_of_item_base_premiums
  + sum_of_curse_surcharges (50 % of cursed items' base)
  + sum_of_high_enchant_surcharges (30 % of high-enchant items' base)
  + sum_of_first_insurance_surcharges (10 % of every item's base)
  − loyalty_discount (20 % of policy base, if applicable)
  − follow_up_discount (15 % of policy base, if applicable)
  + 5 G fee
)

Intermediate amounts are kept as fractions; only the final result
is rounded.

### R3: Claim processing

- **Deductible:** 100 G per damage event (per damaged item entry).
- **Cap:** total payout per policy capped at 2 × policy insurance
  sum. Cap is based on unmodified insurance values.
- **Enchantment ≥ 8 clause:** damage reimbursed at 50 % of damage
  amount (applied before the deductible).
- **Dragon material clause:** damage fully reimbursed.
- When both clauses apply, the 50 % rule wins (then deductible).
- Items without enchantment/material (e.g. runes) get full
  reimbursement minus deductible.

Per-damage payout formula:
1. Start with damage amount.
2. If enchantment ≥ 8 → halve it (50 % clause wins over dragon).
   Else if dragon material → keep full amount.
   Else → keep full amount.
3. Subtract 100 G deductible (floor at 0 for that item).
4. Sum per-damage payouts.
5. Cap the total to remaining cap.
6. Round DOWN to whole G (in the MHPCO's favor).

### R4: Rounding in MHPCO's favor

- Premiums round UP (e.g., 197.5 → 198).
- Payouts round DOWN (e.g., 350.5 → 350).
- Intermediate amounts kept as fractions; rounding only at the end.

### R5: Insurance sum & cap

- Insurance sum = sum of items' raw insurance values (block
  discount does NOT affect insurance sum).
- Cap = 2 × insurance sum.
- Cap is based on unmodified insurance values; premium modifiers do
  not raise the cap.
- Cap is consumed across successive claims on the same policy.

### R6: Multiple items of the same type

- A policy can list two of the same item type (e.g. two swords);
  insurance sum doubles.
- Each `{itemType, ...}` entry in `damages` is a separate damage
  event with its own deductible.
- If `damages` contains more entries of a type than the policy
  covers (e.g. two sword damages but only one sword insured), the
  whole claim is rejected: CLI exits non-zero, error to stderr.

### R7: CLI

- Reads JSON from stdin, writes JSON to stdout.
- Input has `customer` (with `yearsWithMHPCO`) and `steps` array.
- Each step is either a quote (`op: "quote"`, `items`) or claim
  (`op: "claim"`, `policy`, `incident: {cause, damages: [{itemType, amount}]}`).
- Steps processed sequentially; claims reference an earlier quote
  by zero-based step index.
- Output: `{results: [...]}` — same length/order as input steps.
  Quote result: `{premium}`. Claim result: `{payout, remainingCap}`.
- The follow-up surcharge tracks the customer's quote sequence
  within the scenario.
- The CLI executable is invoked via `src/cli.ts`.

### R8: Error handling

- Unknown item type in a quote → exit non-zero, error to stderr, no
  `results` to stdout.
- Claim references a damage entry whose item is not part of the
  policy (or item with unknown type) → exit non-zero, error to
  stderr.
- Damage entry with negative amount → exit non-zero, error to
  stderr.

## Examples

### E1: Building block — 2 runes (no block)
Base premium = 2 × 25 = **50 G**.

### E2: Building block — 3 runes (block applies)
Base premium = **60 G** (replaces 3 × 25).

### E3: Building block — 4 runes (no block; exactly 3 required)
Base premium = 4 × 25 = **100 G**.

### E4: Building block — 7 runes
3 form a block (60 G), 4 remaining at 25 G each = 60 + 100 =
**175 G**.

### E5: "Alike" — 2 runes + 1 moonstone (different types)
No block: 2×25 + 25 = **75 G**.

### E6: "Alike" — 3 runes + 3 moonstones (two separate blocks)
60 + 60 = **120 G**.

### E7: Modifier scope — cursed sword + plain amulet
Item base premiums: 100 + 60 = 160 G.
Curse surcharge: 50 % × 100 = 50 G.
Before further modifiers/fee: **210 G**.

### E8: Loyalty threshold (exactly 2 years)
Loyalty discount applies.

### E9: High-enchantment threshold (exactly 5)
High-enchantment surcharge applies; if also cursed, both apply.

### E10: Low enchantment (4)
No high-enchantment surcharge; curse surcharge only if cursed.

### E11: Dragon sword, enchantment exactly 8, damage 1000 G
Payout = 500 − 100 = **400 G** (50 % rule wins, then deductible).

### E12: Dragon attack, sword (500 G) + amulet (300 G), deductible per item
Payout = (500 − 100) + (300 − 100) = **600 G**.

### E13: Standard sword, enchantment 3, damage 500 G
Payout = 500 − 100 = **400 G**.

### E14: Rune damage 200 G
Payout = 200 − 100 = **100 G**.

### E15: Dragon sword enchantment 9, damage 1000 G
Both clauses; 50 % wins. 500 − 100 = **400 G**.

### E16: Dragon sword enchantment 5, damage 800 G
Only dragon clause applies. 800 − 100 = **700 G**.

### E17: Steel sword enchantment 9, damage 1000 G
Only high-enchant clause. 500 − 100 = **400 G**.

### E18: Two swords policy — insurance sum & cap
Insurance sum 2000 G; cap 4000 G.

### E19: Two-sword policy, dragon attack damages both
Two `{itemType: sword}` entries → each its own deductible.

### E20: Too many damages for what's insured
Two sword damages but only one sword insured → claim rejected,
CLI exits non-zero, error to stderr.

### E21: Cap = 2 × insurance sum (sword + amulet)
Insurance sum = 1600 G; cap = **3200 G**.

### E22: Cap based on unmodified values
Cursed sword (premium 165 G after modifiers): cap still **2000 G**.

### E23: Block discount doesn't affect insurance sum
Sword + 3 runes: insurance sum = 1000 + 3 × 250 = **1750 G**.

### E24: Cap exhaustion across successive claims
Sword (cap 2000 G); two claims of 1500 G each.
- Claim 1: payout = 1400 G (1500 − 100 deductible), cap remaining
  600 G.
- Claim 2: desired 1400 G capped to 600 G remaining. Payout 600 G,
  cap remaining 0 G.

### E25: Rounding — premium 197.5 → 198
### E26: Rounding — payout 350.5 → 350

### E27: Empty item list
Premium = **5 G** (only processing fee).

### E28: Unknown item type in quote
CLI exits non-zero, stderr error, no `results` on stdout.

### E29: Claim references item not in policy
(amulet damaged but only sword insured, OR unknown item type)
→ CLI exits non-zero, stderr error.

### E30: Negative damage amount
→ CLI exits non-zero, stderr error.

### E31: Newcomer integration — cursed sword (steel, enchant 3),
customer 0 years, first contract.
100 (base) + 50 (curse) + 10 (first insurance) + 5 (fee) =
**165 G**.

### E32: Long-standing customer's 2nd contract — cursed sword
(steel, enchant 7), customer 3 years.
100 base + 50 curse + 30 high enchant − 20 loyalty + 10 first
insurance − 15 follow-up + 5 fee = **160 G**.

## Interface contract

The CLI reads JSON from stdin and writes JSON to stdout. Field
names below are normative.

### Input

```json
{
  "customer": {"yearsWithMHPCO": 5},
  "steps": [
    {
      "op": "quote",
      "items": [
        {"type": "amulet", "material": "silver", "enchantment": 2, "cursed": false}
      ]
    },
    {
      "op": "claim",
      "policy": 0,
      "incident": {
        "cause": "fire",
        "damages": [
          {"itemType": "amulet", "amount": 200}
        ]
      }
    }
  ]
}
```

### Output

```json
{
  "results": [
    {"premium": 60},
    {"payout": 100, "remainingCap": 1100}
  ]
}
```

(The exact numbers depend on the inputs; only the **shape** above
is binding.)

- Item types: `"sword" | "amulet" | "staff" | "potion" | "rune" | "moonstone"` (others → error).
- Component types include `"rune"` and `"moonstone"`; other component-like types may exist but the spec names only these two.
- Item properties `material`, `enchantment`, `cursed` are optional; main items default to non-cursed, no enchantment, generic material.
- Components don't have enchantment/material/cursed in any example;
  treat absent properties as default (no surcharge applies).

## Questions / Clarifications

- ❓ "Alike" components → resolved by spec: same TYPE (not family).
  E.g. 2 runes + 1 moonstone is NOT a block. (E5, E6)
- ❓ Cursed surcharge scope → resolved: per-item, not per-policy.
  Curse surcharge = 50 % of the cursed item's base premium. (E7)
- ❓ Multiple items of same type → resolved: allowed; each damage
  entry is treated independently with its own deductible. Too-many
  damages → claim rejected. (E18-20)
- ❓ "First insurance" → resolved: applies per item in each quote,
  regardless of customer history. (E32)

## Per-test rationale

Tests target the `claim-office.ts` module's pure functions
(`quote(customer, items)` returning premium integer, and
`claim(policy, incident, remainingCap)` returning {payout,
remainingCap}). CLI tests exercise the JSON in/out flow including
scenario sequencing, error handling, and exit codes.

Ordered simple → complex:

### Quote function (pure)
1. Empty item list → 5 G fee only (E27, R7-trivial)
2. Single sword, newcomer, no curse, low enchant → 100 + 10 + 5 = 115 G (R1, R2 baseline)
3. Single amulet, newcomer → 60 + 6 + 5 = 71 G (R1)
4. Single staff, newcomer → 80 + 8 + 5 = 93 G (R1)
5. Single potion, newcomer → 40 + 4 + 5 = 49 G (R1)
6. 2 runes, newcomer → 50 base + 5 first ins (10%) + 5 fee = 60 G (E1, R1)
7. 3 runes (block), newcomer → 60 + 6 + 5 = 71 G (E2, R1)
8. 4 runes (no block), newcomer → 100 + 10 + 5 = 115 G (E3, R1)
9. 7 runes → 175 + 17.5 + 5 = 197.5 → 198 G (E4, E25, rounding)
10. 2 runes + 1 moonstone → 75 + 7.5 + 5 = 87.5 → 88 G (E5)
11. 3 runes + 3 moonstones → 120 + 12 + 5 = 137 G (E6)
12. Cursed sword newcomer (integration E31) → 165 G
13. Cursed sword + plain amulet, newcomer → policy base 160, curse +50, first ins 10+6=16, fee 5 → 231 G (E7 integration; isolated value 210 G before further mods+fee documented in spec)
14. Loyalty threshold: exactly 2 years, single sword → 100 base − 20 loyalty + 10 first + 5 fee = 95 G (E8, R2)
15. Loyalty just below: 1 year, single sword → 100 + 10 + 5 = 115 G (no loyalty)
16. High enchant threshold exactly 5: sword enchant 5 → 100 + 30 + 10 + 5 = 145 G (E9)
17. High enchant just below 4: sword enchant 4 → 100 + 10 + 5 = 115 G (E10)
18. Cursed + enchant 5 sword → 100 + 50 + 30 + 10 + 5 = 195 G (E9 combined)
19. Long-standing customer 2nd contract integration (E32) → 160 G
20. Premium rounded up — confirms 197.5 → 198 (covered by test 9, also E25)

### Claim function (pure)
21. Standard sword damage 500, no special → 400 (E13)
22. Rune damage 200 → 100 (E14)
23. Dragon sword enchant 8, damage 1000 → 400 (E11, 50% wins)
24. Dragon sword enchant 9, damage 1000 → 400 (E15)
25. Dragon sword enchant 5, damage 800 → 700 (E16)
26. Steel sword enchant 9, damage 1000 → 400 (E17)
27. Dragon attack: sword 500 + amulet 300 → 600 (E12)
28. Two swords policy, dragon attack two swords damages: each its own deductible (E19)
29. Cap = 2 × insurance sum (sword + amulet → 3200) (E21)
30. Cap unaffected by premium modifiers (cursed sword cap still 2000) (E22)
31. Block discount doesn't affect insurance sum: sword + 3 runes → 1750, cap 3500 (E23)
32. Cap exhaustion across two claims: 1500 → 1400 (cap 600), 1500 → 600 (cap 0) (E24)
33. Payout rounded down — 350.5 → 350 (E26) — construct a half-G scenario via 50% clause: dragon irrelevant; sword enchant 8 damage 901 → halve to 450.5, minus 100 → 350.5 → 350.

### CLI (JSON in/out)
34. CLI quote round-trip with schema example → {results: [{premium: N}]}
35. CLI scenario: quote then claim referencing policy index 0 → results array with both
36. CLI follow-up tracking: two quotes, second gets follow-up discount (E32 scenario)
37. CLI unknown item type in quote → exit non-zero, stderr error, no results (E28)
38. CLI claim references item not in policy → exit non-zero, stderr error (E29)
39. CLI negative damage amount → exit non-zero, stderr error (E30)
40. CLI too-many-damages (E20) → exit non-zero, stderr error

content.text:
Wait — let me reconsider the integration for E7 (the cursed sword + plain amulet example). The spec says explicitly: "the cursed surcharge adds 50 G ... → 210 G before further modifiers and fee." That phrasing means the 210 G is policy base + curse surcharge only, before first-insurance surcharge, loyalty/follow-up, and fee. So my "integrated" computation was right, but let me double-check the math: 160 base + 50 curse + 10 first-ins(sword) + 6 first-ins(amulet) + 5 fee = 231 G. That's the full quoted premium for a newcomer with no loyalty.

Now let me write the test file.

content.content: File created successfully at: /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-22_08-52-17_claim-office-example-mapping_v4.2-shared-context_opus-4-7-portkey-no-thinking/src/claim-office.spec.ts (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

content.text:
The current task is straightforward (create the example mapping file and test list — both done). Task tracking isn't really beneficial here. Let me verify the spec is solid for the tricky test (test 9: 7 runes).

Checking math for 7 runes:
- 3 form a block (60 G), 4 remaining at 25 G each (100 G) → base = 160 G... wait, that conflicts with the spec which says 7 runes → 175 G base premium.

Let me re-read: "7 runes → 175 G base premium". So 7 runes = 175 G. Let me check: if block of 3 = 60, and remaining 4 at 25 = 100, total = 160. But spec says 175. Hmm.

Wait — maybe only ONE block per group, and the rest are individual: 175 = 60 + 4×25 + ?. No, that's 160. Or: 7 × 25 = 175 G (no block applied at all because exactly 3 required?). Let me re-read: "3 runes → 60 G base premium (block applies)" and "4 runes → 100 G base premium (no block — block requires exactly 3)". So the block only triggers at exactly 3. With 7 runes you don't have "exactly 3", so all 7 are billed at 25 G = 175 G. That makes sense!

So my description "60 block + 4×25" was wrong. Let me fix the test descriptions for 7 runes.

input.old_string: it.todo("7 runes → premium 198 G (60 block + 4×25 = 160 wait — 175 base + 17.5 first-insurance + 5 fee = 197.5 rounded up to 198)");
input.new_string: it.todo("7 runes (block requires exactly 3, so all 7 billed individually) → premium 198 G (175 base + 17.5 first-insurance + 5 fee = 197.5 rounded up to 198)");

content.text: Also need to fix the example mapping file E4:

input.old_string:
### E4: Building block — 7 runes
3 form a block (60 G), 4 remaining at 25 G each = 60 + 100 =
**175 G**.
input.new_string:
### E4: Building block — 7 runes
The block requires **exactly 3** alike components. 7 ≠ 3, so no
block applies; all 7 are billed individually: 7 × 25 = **175 G**.

(Note: the block is a one-shot pricing rule for a group of exactly
3 — it does not "decompose" larger quantities into blocks + leftovers.)

content.content: The file /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-22_08-52-17_claim-office-example-mapping_v4.2-shared-context_opus-4-7-portkey-no-thinking/example-mapping/claim-office.md has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

content.text:
Wait — but then 3 runes + 3 moonstones = 120 G (E6) implies "two separate blocks" — so two groups of exactly 3 each. The spec confirms: with 3-of-the-same, the block applies. So "exactly 3 of a given type" triggers the block. For 7 runes (>3 of same type), no block. For 3+3 (two groups of exactly 3, of different types), two blocks. OK, my updated interpretation is consistent.

Let me also verify test 33 (payout rounding). I claimed: sword enchant 8, damage 901 → halve to 450.5 − 100 = 350.5 → 350. That works. But wait, the spec says the 50% rule applies to "items with enchantment level ≥ 8". A sword has integer damage 901? Per the schema, `amount` is an integer. Good.

Let me also double-check test 28 (two swords dragon attack). The spec says: "a dragon attack damages both swords; `damages` contains two `{itemType: "sword", ...}` entries → each entry is treated as a separate damage with its own deductible". I should pick concrete numbers. Let me make the test description concrete:

content.text:
All 40 tests are present as todos and the file parses cleanly.

## Test List Created

**Feature**: MHPCO Claim Office (quote + claim operations, CLI)
**Test File**: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-22_08-52-17_claim-office-example-mapping_v4.2-shared-context_opus-4-7-portkey-no-thinking/src/claim-office.spec.ts`
**Example Mapping**: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-22_08-52-17_claim-office-example-mapping_v4.2-shared-context_opus-4-7-portkey-no-thinking/example-mapping/claim-office.md`
**Tests**: 40 (all `it.todo()`)

**Test groups (ordered simple → complex)**:

1. quote — base price list and processing fee (5 tests): empty list, sword, amulet, staff, potion
2. quote — component building block (6 tests): 2/3/4/7 runes, mixed types, two-block scenario
3. quote — modifier thresholds (5 tests): loyalty at/below 2y, high-enchant at/below 5, combined cursed+high-enchant
4. quote — modifier scope on multi-item policies (1 test): cursed sword + plain amulet (E7)
5. quote — integration examples (2 tests): newcomer cursed sword (E31, 165 G), long-standing 2nd contract (E32, 160 G)
6. claim — standard reimbursement (2 tests): regular sword damage 500→400, rune damage 200→100
7. claim — enchantment & dragon-material clauses (4 tests): E11, E15, E16, E17
8. claim — deductible per damage event (2 tests): mixed-type dragon attack, two-sword dragon attack
9. claim — insurance sum and cap (4 tests): cap formula, cap unaffected by premium modifiers, block doesn't reduce sum, cap exhaustion across claims
10. rounding (2 tests): premium 197.5→198, payout 350.5→350
11. CLI — JSON in/out (3 tests): basic round-trip, quote+claim sequencing, follow-up tracking
12. CLI — error handling (4 tests): unknown item type, claim references unknown item, negative damage, too-many damages

**Key resolutions captured in example-mapping**:
- "Alike components" → same TYPE (E5, E6)
- Cursed surcharge → per-item, not policy-wide (E7)
- Block requires exactly 3 of same type — does NOT decompose larger groups (7 runes → 7×25=175, confirmed by spec)
- First-insurance surcharge → per item every quote, regardless of customer history (E32)
- All error paths from spec covered with CLI tests (E28, E29, E30, E20)

**Next Step**: Proceed to Red phase to activate the first test.
